markdown:
# Ishredon

## World Overview

Ishredon is a grounded fantasy world of roads, market squares, shipyards, shrines, fortresses, mills, mines, and difficult frontiers. Most lives here are shaped less by prophecy than by weather, work, obligation, and distance. Grain must be brought in, bridges repaired, tolls argued over, roads patrolled, contracts honored, and winter outlasted. It is a world where a farmer may never see a spell cast in their lifetime, yet may still hear an orc chorus in a harbor hall, buy a precision instrument from Nex, or look up from a field and watch the engines of Auromass pass overhead.

Magic is real in Ishredon, but it no longer governs the age. The great eras of war-wizards, summoned horrors, dragon banners, and sorcerous empire survive mostly through ruins, songs, and old fear. Power has not vanished, but it has changed hands. Where magic receded, craft, law, logistics, and industry advanced. A sealed granary, a fortified bridge, a working pump, a navigable pass, or a reliable charter may now matter more to a province than any miracle.

This makes Ishredon a world of strong regional character. Each province has its own idea of order, wealth, and civilization. Landred values stability, harvest, and the maintenance of settled life. Ostrengarr binds art, memory, and public life into one civic culture. Fildren tests the limits of survival in the shadow of the dead. Nex prizes permanence, precision, and the discipline of making things properly. Auromass, most ambitious of all, has turned engineering into a public creed and built a realm in the sky without surrendering its faith in ledgers, labor, and law.

Despite their differences, the provinces are tightly entangled. Landred feeds much of the continent. Ostrengarr sends timber, spice, dyes, ships, and seaborne influence abroad. Nex provides metals, tools, engines, and expertise on which others depend. Auromass reshapes trade, status, and warfare through flight and infrastructure. Fildren exports little, yet burdens all with its threat, its mysteries, and the memory of what waits when civilization fails to hold its borders. Ishredon is therefore not merely a collection of distinct lands, but a continent in tension: old powers fading, new systems rising, and every province convinced it understands the future better than the others.

## Provinces of Ishredon

Ishredon is split into five main provinces:

### Landred
Landred is the breadbasket of Ishredon: a fertile, deeply settled province of hedgerows, broad fields, orchard country, market towns, parish shrines, old bridges, and roads busy with carts, drovers, pilgrims, and tax men. It is the most outwardly familiar of the provinces and, for that reason, among the easiest to underestimate. Life here turns on planting, rent, tolls, fairs, river depth, inheritance, and whether stores will hold through winter.

Its great strength is density. Villages lie near enough to roads, towns, and neighboring fields that no part of Landred feels truly wild for long. Yet this order is maintained rather than effortless. Noble houses contest land and privilege, abbeys guard influence, river tolls cause long resentment, and every boundary hedge marks some older agreement or grievance. Landred feeds the continent, and because it feeds the continent, much depends on it remaining stable.

### Ostrengarr
Ostrengarr lies in the warmer middle of Ishredon, a province of rainforest roads, river basins, storm-heavy coasts, tidal inlets, bright beaches, painted halls, and rich trade in spice, fruit, hardwood, dyes, and seaborne goods. It is the ancestral homeland of the orcs, though many peoples live and work in its ports, craft towns, and inland settlements.

Its defining feature is not climate alone, but culture. Ostrengarr is a place where music, public speech, memory, and performance carry genuine civic force. Under the legacy of Wild Fritz Aled, law and reputation often move through ceremony as much as proclamation. Outsiders who expect only old warlike legend find instead a prosperous, self-assured province that treats beauty, hospitality, and discipline not as opposites, but as parts of the same social order.

### Fildren
Fildren is the far north of Ishredon: a desolate province of snowbound plains, black pine, frozen marsh, ruined forts, abandoned roads, and winters long enough to erase confidence. It is the sparsest of the provinces, not because no one ever tried to settle it, but because the cold, the distance, and the dead drove most such efforts into history.

It remains a haunted frontier. The undead are said to move there without complaint or hunger, and enough of those stories are true that the whole continent treats the north with dread. Fildren still holds a few surviving settlements, outposts, shrines, and ports, but they endure rather than thrive. It exports little beyond warning, rumor, and relics, yet its presence shapes every southern imagination of what lies beyond the last maintained road.

### Nex
Nex is a province of mountains, lochs, ravines, cave systems, gate towns, and vast inhabited under-realms. Populated chiefly by dwarves, gnomes, and their close kindred, it is one of the richest regions in Ishredon not merely in ore or gems, but in technique, infrastructure, and disciplined craftsmanship. Surface travelers see hard weather, cranes, terraces, toll tunnels, and stocky stone settlements. Beneath that lies the deeper reality of reservoirs, lantern galleries, liftworks, forge districts, archive vaults, and ancient engineered halls.

Nex is admired because everyone depends on it. Metals, tools, machines, surveying instruments, clockwork systems, and technical knowledge radiate outward from its holds into every other province. It is also resented for the same reason. Nex values proof, precision, inheritance of skill, and things made to last. Its marvels are rarely flamboyant, but they are foundational.

### Auromass
Auromass is the most unusual province in Ishredon, divided between an industrious groundside state and a vast skyside dominion suspended above it. Its lower towns are full of foundries, ropewalks, machine halls, signal towers, gasworks, sail lofts, legal chambers, and training schools. It is one of the most mixed and civically self-conscious provinces in the world, governed through charters, councils, guild influence, and fiercely defended legal equality.

The sky realm of Auromass is among the greatest wonders of the age, held aloft not by sorcery but by engines, airships, liftworks, anchors, maintenance crews, and an extraordinary tolerance for paperwork. Its terraces, gardens, docks, and elevated districts are breathtaking, but never separate from the systems that sustain them. More than any other province, Auromass embodies the new age of Ishredon: ambitious, mechanical, argumentative, and convinced that craft and organization can achieve what older eras left to magic.
